chunk_summary: Guidance on bottle weaning, night feed reduction, and oral health practices for children aged 9–24 months, emphasizing gradual transitions, oral hygiene, and dental care starting with the first tooth.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of bottle weaning by 12–18 months to prevent early childhood caries and improve appetite.
Learn strategies for reducing night milk feeds while ensuring adequate daytime nutrition.
Implement oral hygiene practices, including brushing with fluoridated toothpaste and scheduling the first dental visit by 12 months.
key_facts: Bottle cessation should ideally be completed by 12–18 months using a staged approach.
Start brushing with the first tooth using a rice-grain-sized smear of fluoridated toothpaste for children under 3 years.
Night milk reduction is appropriate when growth is normal and daytime intake is adequate; reduce gradually by shortening duration/volume every few nights.
Avoid bottle propping and carrying bottles around the house to reduce choking and aspiration risks.
Recommend the first dental visit by 12 months or within 6 months of the first tooth eruption.
reference_quote: Start brushing with the first tooth, twice daily, using a smear of fluoridated toothpaste (rice-grain sized amount) in children under 3 years, with adult assistance. — American Academy of Pediatric Dentistry
document_key_topics: Bottle weaning strategies for children under 2 years
Night feed reduction and appetite improvement
Oral hygiene practices and dental care guidelines
